learningObjectives: Understand that database schemas evolve over time to meet new requirements.
Differentiate between non-destructive and destructive database schema changes.
Identify common SQL commands used for non-destructive schema changes, such as adding columns or tables.
Explain the implications of adding a NULLable column to an existing table.
Analyze an Entity-Relationship Diagram (ERD) to understand table relationships.
hook: Imagine you run a hospital. How do you store 10,000 patients without duplication, and what happens when you need to add new information, like their preferred contact method, to every patient's record?
misconceptions: Schema changes are always simple and risk-free.
Adding a new column automatically requires updating all existing rows with non-NULL values.
All schema changes can be done without impacting existing applications.
Destructive changes are just 'bad' changes and should always be avoided.

instructionalProcedures.title: The Evolving Database: Why Change is Constant
instructionalProcedures.description: Start with the real-world hook to illustrate the need for database management and schema evolution. Introduce the concept that databases are not static and must adapt to changing needs, referencing the idea of maintaining a database as systems evolve.
teacherActions: Present the hospital scenario and ask students to consider data storage challenges.
Introduce the lesson topic: 'Database Schema Changes' using the title region.
Explain that databases are continuously evolving, referencing 'out how to maintain a database when the system is continuously evolving' (p.2).
studentActions: Engage with the hospital scenario, brainstorming initial ideas for data storage.
Reflect on the dynamic nature of real-world data systems.
Understand the learning objectives for the session.

instructionalProcedures.title: Understanding a Database Schema: The Blueprint
instructionalProcedures.description: Introduce the concept of a database schema as the blueprint. Present an ERD to visualize a database structure, walking through each table and its purpose individually before discussing relationships.
teacherActions: Define 'database schema' using 'A database consists of schema and data' (p.9).
Display the ERD from p1_r3.
Walk through each table (Album, Artist, Photo, Member, Comment) in the ERD, explaining its columns and primary keys.
Introduce foreign keys and how they link tables, using examples from the ERD.
studentActions: Examine the ERD and identify different tables.
Understand the role of primary and foreign keys.
Trace simple relationships between tables based on the ERD.

instructionalProcedures.title: Schema Changes: Non-Destructive vs. Destructive
instructionalProcedures.description: Explain the two main categories of schema changes: non-destructive and destructive, using direct evidence from the PDF. Provide concrete examples of non-destructive changes, such as adding a column or a new table, and discuss their implications.
teacherActions: Introduce 'Types of Database Changes' (p.11).
Explain that 'Every schema change falls into one of two categories: Non-destructive and Destructive' (p.11).
Detail 'Example A — Adding a column (Non-destructive)' with the user story: 'Users should be able to save their preferred language.' (p.11).
Show the SQL: 'ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN preferred_language VARCHAR(10) NULL;' (p.11).
Explain the 'NULL means old rows are unaffected. Old code ignores the column. New code uses it.' (p.11) and 'Completely safe — no existing table is touched.' (p.11).
Detail 'Example B — Adding a new table (Non-destructive)' with the user story: 'Track which products are on a user's wishlist.' (p.11).
Briefly mention 'Destructive changes must be handled with a 3-phase approach' (p.11) as a contrast.
studentActions: Distinguish between non-destructive and destructive changes.
Analyze the `ALTER TABLE` SQL syntax for adding a column.
Understand why adding a NULLable column is considered non-destructive and safe.
Recognize that adding a new, independent table is also non-destructive.

instructionalProcedures.title: Practicing Non-Destructive Changes and Querying
instructionalProcedures.description: Students will apply their understanding by performing a simulated non-destructive change and then querying the modified schema. This phase includes a dry run of a query to show how data is retrieved after a schema modification.
teacherActions: Provide a scenario: 'Add a column `email_verified` (BOOLEAN, NULL) to the `Member` table in our ERD (p1_r3).'
Guide students to write the `ALTER TABLE` statement for this change.
Show a `SELECT` query that includes the newly added `email_verified` column from the `Member` table.
Perform a `query_dry_run` to trace how the database engine processes the query, showing NULLs for existing rows.
Discuss common mistakes related to `NULL` handling when querying newly added columns, emphasizing that `NULL` is not `0` or an empty string.
studentActions: Write an `ALTER TABLE` statement to add a new column to an existing table.
Construct a `SELECT` query to retrieve data, including a newly added column.
Follow the `query_dry_run` to understand query execution.
Identify and correct potential errors related to `NULL` values in queries.

instructionalProcedures.title: Assessing Schema Change Understanding
instructionalProcedures.description: Students will be challenged to identify errors in SQL statements related to schema changes and to propose a non-destructive change for a new user requirement. This assesses their ability to apply the concepts learned.
teacherActions: Present a broken `ALTER TABLE` statement and ask students to 'spot the mistake' related to `NULL` constraints or data types.
Pose a challenge: 'A user wants to track the 'creation_date' for each 'Photo' in our ERD (p1_r3). Write the SQL to add this column non-destructively.'
Provide feedback on student challenge queries.
Offer a multiple-choice question on the definition or characteristics of non-destructive changes.
studentActions: Analyze a given SQL query to identify and correct errors.
Formulate an `ALTER TABLE` statement to meet a new requirement, ensuring it is non-destructive.
Self-assess their understanding of schema change types and SQL syntax.

instructionalProcedures.title: Recap: Adapting Your Database
instructionalProcedures.description: Summarize the key takeaways regarding schema evolution and the distinction between non-destructive and destructive changes, reinforcing the importance of careful planning.
teacherActions: Recap the main points: databases evolve, schema changes are categorized as non-destructive or destructive.
Reiterate the safety and common use cases for non-destructive changes (adding columns with NULL, adding new tables).
Emphasize the importance of understanding these concepts for maintaining a robust database system.
studentActions: Review the key concepts covered in the lesson.
Articulate the main differences between non-destructive and destructive schema changes.
Confirm their understanding of how to perform basic non-destructive schema modifications.

differentiationStrategies: Provide scaffolded support for writing SQL queries, offering hints or partial solutions for students struggling with syntax.
Offer advanced challenges for quicker learners, such as considering how to migrate data for a new non-NULLable column.
Use visual aids like `before_after` screens to clearly illustrate the impact of schema changes.
Allow students to work in pairs for the challenge activities to foster peer learning.
assessmentPlan: Formative assessment through 'spot the mistake' activities on `ALTER TABLE` statements.
Observation of student participation and problem-solving during guided practice and challenge activities.
A final challenge where students propose and write SQL for a non-destructive schema change based on a new user story.
Quick multiple-choice questions to check understanding of key terms and concepts like 'non-destructive' and 'NULL'.
closureActivity: Students will complete a 'one-minute summary' explaining the two main types of schema changes and providing an example of a non-destructive change they learned today.
